availability.p: Published under a Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 16

p.del: The opinion of Innocent, therefore, is to be understood as we have said above. Otherwise it would follow that if an entire kingdom or the entire world — such as the Indies, where there are thousands upon thousands, and hundreds of hundreds of thousands of generations — were found to be infected with unnatural crimes (which we do not believe to be true), or given over to idolatry (and this we believe to be certainly true for the greater part), then all the inhabitants thereof, who surpass the number of the stars, would have to be consumed by fire and sword — which is utterly foreign and utterly absurd to the spirit and teaching of Christ, by whose blood they were redeemed.

div.p: Therefore, when Innocent said that if any pagan should sin against the law of nature or worship idols, he could be punished by the pope by example of divine judgment, it was not his intention to speak of all gentiles or unbelievers absolutely, but only of those who inhabit lands or kingdoms in which the Christian name once existed, and where it is known with certainty that true divine worship flourished. Otherwise, Innocent would have written most culpably. Moreover, the argument drawn from avoiding absurdity, or from the reasoning of absurdity, inhumanity, or inconvenience to be avoided, is most powerful in law, since it is derived from natural discernment and holds even when no authority is cited.

And it is formulated in this way: one must not assert that, nor adopt that interpretation, from which absurdity, inhumanity, or inconvenience could follow. For every provision ought to be understood in such a way that no inhumanity or absurdity follows, because absurdity must always be avoided. Hence it is that in order to avoid absurdity, a general provision must be restricted — .l. 2. C. de noxialibus actionibus, and .l. vt gradatim, §.1. ff. de muneribus et honoribus — even statutory provisions, as Bartolus says in l. omnes populi, in the 6th principal question, ff. de iustitia et iure. On this argument, sufficient is written and noted in c. venerabilem and c. genesi: de electione, and in c. solite de maioritate et obedientia, and in c. ex ore, de priuilegiis, and in both these places by the doctors, and in countless other places of both canon and civil law.

Since, therefore, in the statements of so great a man, namely Innocent, so many and such great inhumane, absurd, and inconvenient conclusions must rightly be avoided, whatever he may have said in this article concerning unbelievers must necessarily be restricted to pertain only to the reasoning of those who (as we have already said) defile with the filth of idolatry or of impurities those places that have long belonged to the Church. And that this is indeed the case, beyond the absurdity that follows, is proven as follows.

First: because when kingdoms that once belonged to Christians are possessed by unbelievers and are defiled by abominable superstitions, an injury is done to Christ, to whom those kingdoms were once dedicated and consecrated to divine worship; and since, according to the rule of law, what has once been dedicated to him may not thereafter be transferred to human uses, much less to impious superstitions and wicked and disgraceful crimes. For what does it matter if Muhammad is worshipped there, or empty idols instead of the true God?

Therefore the Church ought not to tolerate or overlook such things, but — if she has the capacity — either compel the unbelievers to restore those places to her, or forbid or even punish such defilements from occurring, on account of their foulness and gravity, for the reasons and authorities cited above. And that this was the intention of Innocent is proven from his own words; for speaking of the indulgences granted to soldiers going to recover the Holy Land, he subjoins the reason: that it was consecrated by the birth, dwelling, and death of Jesus Christ, and that in it Christ is not worshipped but Muhammad; the pope may justly reclaim it so that it may be inhabited by Christians. Thus says he.

¶ Second: because, granted that those provinces were once dedicated and consecrated to Christ, they now belong by right to the Church; and thus all unbelievers dwelling there are subject to the Church and to its laws, of which they are nevertheless capable by reason of domicile. For one dwelling outwardly in the land of a foreign prince becomes that prince's subject, as in c. ex parte et in c. dilecti and c. finali de foro competenti, from C. vbi petatur tutor l. 1, and as is there noted by the doctors, and ff. de iudiciis l. heres absens § proinde. Therefore, in provinces that were once under Christian rule, the Church has a particular and special reason — more than in other places — to both prohibit and punish impious superstitions and wicked crimes.

¶ Third: because if in those kingdoms Christians dwell under the yoke of barbarians, there is a greater reproach to the Christian name; and Christians themselves, living alongside unbelievers, are exposed to the danger of being defiled by their impiety. For evil company corrupts good morals, and the association of wicked men often corrupts even the good — 28. q. 1. c. Sepe — how much more so those who are inclined toward vices — 24. q. 3, c. resecandae and c. sed illud. 45. distinctio, for these reasons.

Speaking of such unbelievers, Innocent rightly teaches that the pope can prescribe laws to them lest they trouble Christians; and that if they afflict Christians with injuries, the pope can deprive such unbelievers of kingdoms that were once under Christian rule. He adds, however, that a great cause must precede before the pontiff takes such action, on account of the great strife and tumult that would necessarily arise from it — provided that Christians are not put in danger. Now, "danger" is to be understood as present when there is risk that Christians might fall away from the Catholic faith, or some similar danger. For in that case, lest such a thing come to pass, all other things must be set aside.

¶ Innocent likewise teaches that such unbelievers must permit the Gospel to be preached in their kingdoms; otherwise, since they sin in this matter, they can be punished by the pope. But more on this below.

In sum, the words of Innocent — namely, that if a pagan acts against the law of nature — are to be understood (as we have taught above) either of unbelievers who inhabit lands that were formerly Christian, or of unbelievers who are actually and by right subject to Christian princes — as is expressly stated in c. archiepiscopatu, de raptoribus, where a certain archbishop of Sicily punishes Moors who sin against nature. Or it is to be understood in the manner I taught above, namely that they are subject only by right in that they hold usurped lands of the Church, by reason of the three special grounds stated. And thus are to be understood the teachings given by Innocent and the Abbot in the Decretals, c. in archiepiscopatu, de raptoribus, and in c. nouit, de iudiciis, and all the canonists who follow them.
